original_abstract: Multimodal visual information fusion aims to integrate data from diverse sensors into a single representation that maximizes complementary information while minimizing redundancy. However, extracting such information remains a significant challenge, particularly for infrared and visible images due to the substantial modality gap. Conventional cross-attention modules prioritize correlation, yet effective image fusion requires a deliberate focus on complementarity—or uncorrelation. To address this, we propose a novel Cross-Attention Mechanism (CAM) specifically designed to enhance complementary feature extraction. Our approach utilizes a two-stage training strategy: first, independent auto-encoder networks are trained for each modality; second, the encoders are frozen while the CAM and a shared decoder are trained to synthesize the final image. By leveraging the CAM, features from both modalities are integrated into a unified representation that amplifies unique information and suppresses redundant noise. Experimental results demonstrate that our proposed method achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple benchmarks. The source code will be made publicly available.
humanized_abstract: Image and video fusion aims to combine the complementary information from multiple sources into a single representation by exploiting their redundancies and complementary aspects, respectively. However, the extraction of complementary information from different sources, such as infrared and visible images, is a challenging task due to the large modality gap between them. Most of the existing cross-attention-based methods rely heavily on the correlation between different modalities, and even focusing on complementary information in image fusion is often overlooked, which leads to unsatisfactory performance. To explore the complementary information from different modalities, a new Cross-Attention Mechanism (CAM) is designed. We adopt a two-stage training approach: 1) training independent auto-encoder networks for different modalities, 2) training the CAM and a shared decoder network with the pre-trained encoders. By using CAM, the complementary features of different modalities can be fully fused into one representation for further improvement. Our experimental results show that the proposed method achieves the new state-of-the-art performance compared with the existing works on several benchmark datasets. The code will be open to the public.

However, the extraction of complementary information from different sources, such as infrared and visible images, is a challenging task due to the large modality gap between them. Most of the existing cross-attention-based methods rely heavily on the correlation between different modalities, and even focusing on complementary information in image fusion is often overlooked, which leads to unsatisfactory performance. To explore the complementary information from different modalities, a new Cross-Attention Mechanism (CAM) is designed. We adopt a two-stage training approach: 1) training independent auto-encoder networks for different modalities, 2) training the CAM and a shared decoder network with the pre-trained encoders. By using CAM, the complementary features of different modalities can be fully fused into one representation for further improvement. Our experimental results show that the proposed method achieves the new state-of-the-art performance compared with the existing works on several benchmark datasets. The code will be open to the public.
